data.title: The Gabelli Utilities Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
THE GABELLI UTILITIES FUND

The Gabelli Utilities Fund (MUTF:GABUX) is a diversified fund whose investment objectives are long term growth of capital and income. The Fund invests in companies that provide products, services, or equipment for the generation or distribution of electricity, gas, and water. Additionally, the Fund will invest in companies in telecommunications services or infrastructure services.

In the first quarter of 2025, the S&P Utilities Index (SPU) returned 4.9% compared to the S&P 500 Composite return of -4.3%. Regulated utilities outperformed non-regulated power companies and the overall market, primarily due to their defensive characteristics. Utilities are insulated from potential tariff impacts while benefiting from lower interest rates, manufacturing onshoring, and increased energy demand. The median first quarter 2025 return of our universe of 50 purely regulated electric, gas, and water utilities was an even stronger 9.0%.

After two decades of flat growth, U.S. electric demand is set for its fastest growth since the 1950s to 1970s era. The mega-cap tech companies (hyperscalers) are aggressively courting electric utilities to build the infrastructure to power mega-data centers, some of which use as much power as small cities. In addition, ongoing electrification and manufacturing onshoring are adding to electric demand. Regulated electric utilities benefit from selling existing power capacity, adding additional power capacity (including batteries), and upgrading/ expanding the transmission and distribution network. Natural gas utility and midstream companies benefit from an expected increase in gas demand to power growth.

Politicians and public utility regulators support the investment for economic and tax base reasons, but also the greater demand helps overall customer affordability. The Trump administration has adopted a more economical approach to meeting electric demand, which means more gas-fired generation, nuclear development, and ongoing renewable development. We expect the relaxation of some environmental and regulatory rules to help infrastructure development. However, potential changes to the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) clean energy tax credits continue to concern investors in clean energy stocks. Utilities have shifted to expansion mode, driving higher capital investment, rate base growth, EPS growth, and consolidation.

Our near-term utility outlook is favorable, based on the potential for higher EPS growth amid a slowing economy and a lower yield curve. Finally, the value of existing infrastructure has become more attractive to potential buyers and consolidation activity has picked up.

Year-to-date, some of the Fund's top contributors were National Fuel Gas (NFG, 7.8%; 31.4%), American Electric Power (AEP, 3.9%; 19.6%), WEC Energy Group (WEC, 3.4%; 16.9%), and Ameren (AEE, 3.0%; 13.5%).

Portfolio detractors included Edison International (EIX, 0.9%; -25.4%), Cameco Corp. (CCJ, 0.7%; -19.9%), and AES Corp. (AES, 2.5%; -2.0%).

*Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025.

LET'S TALK STOCKS

Evergy, Inc. (3.7% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (EVRG - $68.95 - NASDAQ), based in Kansas City, Missouri, is an electric and gas utility serving 1.6 million electric customers in central and eastern Kansas, including the cities of Topeka, Lawrence, Manhattan, Hutchinson, and Wichita and in western Missouri, including Kansas City. In mid-2018, Westar Energy and Great Plains Energy combined to form Evergy. EVRG owns a 15,400 MW generation portfolio (5,900 MWs of coal, 4,100 MW's gas, 4,400 MW's wind, 1,100 nuclear). EVRG expects to earn the top-end of its 4%-to-6% EPS CAGR (from 2025 EPS of $4.02). We believe the growth rate is conservative and will likely be raised. EVRG is methodically integrating strong potential economic load growth (11.2GW pipeline) into sales, rate base, and eventually EPS growth. EVRG's current forecast assumes 2%-3% load growth CAGR through 2029 based on 500-MW's of new load. The forecast does not include 1.6 GW's of load in the "Finalizing Agreements" stage of its economic development pipeline. The solidified 2026-2029 load growth is from 500-MW of the 800MW under active construction, including a $1 billion data center, due in 2028; a $4 billion manufacturing plant for Panasonic, which will produce 4000 new jobs, due in 2026; and an $800 million data center for Meta, due in 2027.

National Fuel Gas Co. (7.5%) (NFG - $79.19 - NYSE), based in Williamsville, New York, is a gas and pipeline utility with a growing exploration and production business. The gas utility serves 754,000 customers in Buffalo, New York, and Erie and Sharon, Pennsylvania. The pipeline & storage (P&S) business operates 3,000 miles of pipe and 34 storage facilities, primarily in the state of New York. The E&P business, Seneca Resources, operates in Appalachia (owns 1.2 million net acres), primarily the Marcellus and Utica shales. Seneca's proved gas reserves at year-end FY 2024 were 4,753 Bcfe (compared to 4,536 Bcfe in FY 2023). Forward gas prices have risen due to growing LNG exports and growing electric power demand. NFG is using free cash flow to buyback stock, improve credit profile, and overall financial strength. NFG raised the annual dividend for the 54th consecutive year to $2.06 per share from $1.98 per share.

NextEra Energy Inc.'s (7.6%) (NEE - $70.89 - NYSE) primary subsidiary, Florida Power & Light (FP&L), based in Juno Beach, Florida, is the largest electric utility in the state, and NextEra Energy Resources (NER), is the nation's leading renewable owner and operator. FP&L serves 5.8 million customers in Eastern, Southern, and Central-Western Florida (35 GWs of generation: 73% gas, 14% nuclear, 14% other). NER owns 34 GWs of net generation, including the nation's largest wind portfolio. NEE owns 101.4 million common units (~58%) of XPLR Infrastructure (XIFR-8.97-HOLD). NEE continues to expect to earn at or near the top of its 6-8% growth rate from 2025-2027.

Southwest Gas Holdings Inc. (3.2%) (SWX - $71.80 - NYSE) is a natural gas utility serving 2.1 million customers in geographically diverse portions of Arizona (1.132 million, or 53%), Nevada (790,000, or 37%), and California (201,000, or 10%). Centuri is an infrastructure utility services contractor specializing in underground gas utility piping, electric distribution, and 5G data. Icahn owns over 10% of shares and was influential in placing four board members on the company's eleven person board. On April 22, 2024, SWX issued 14.3 million shares of Centuri at $21 per share and privately-placed 2.6 million shares to Icahn. Proceeds of $329 million will be used to paydown Centuri debt. SWX owns 81% of the outstanding 88.5 million shares, valued at roughly $1.08 billion, or $15 per SWX share. SWX plans to divest its stake through various options, including a sale of CTRI shares, a distribution to SWX shareholders, a potential exchange of CTRI shares for SWX shares or some combination. SWX would then be a fully regulated gas utility operating in the fast growing states of Arizona and Nevada as well as in California.

data.title: ChargePoint: Weak Quarter And Disappointing Outlook - Sell (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Note:

I have covered ChargePoint Holdings, Inc. or "ChargePoint" (NYSE:CHPT), previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier article on the company.

After the close of Wednesday's session, electric vehicle ("EV") charging solutions provider ChargePoint Holdings reported weaker-than-expected Q1/FY2026 results, with sales coming in below consensus estimates and substantial cash burn:

While higher-margin subscription revenues were up 14% on a year-over-year basis, sales of charging systems deteriorated to new multi-year lows:

On the conference call, management pointed to weakness in Germany as the primary reason behind the revenue shortfall.

On the flip side, consolidated non-GAAP gross margin of 31% reached a new multi-year high:

On the call, management attributed persistent improvement to increased margins in both hardware and subscription, as well as subscription sales growing as a percentage of total revenue.

ChargePoint does not anticipate tariffs to have a material impact on costs of goods sold. As a result, gross margins are expected to continue their upward trajectory later this year.

Disappointingly, operating expenses increased materially on a sequential basis which management attributed to "annual raises and investments in certain key areas of the business":

The combination of lower sales and higher operating expenses resulted in a sequential drop in Adjusted EBITDA:

While inventories were up slightly from the end of fiscal year 2025, the increase was solely a result of currency fluctuations. The company continues to expect inventory to decrease over the course of FY2026.

After burning $34 million in cash during the quarter, ChargePoint ended Q1 with $196 million in unrestricted cash and $300 million in debt. On the call, management pointed to a number of large annual payments as the primary reason for the substantial sequential increase in cash usage.

While the company retains access to its up to $150 million revolving credit facility, harsh minimum liquidity covenants effectively prevent ChargePoint from drawing under the facility.

Unfortunately, market conditions remain challenging as evidenced by the company's disappointing second quarter guidance with sales projected to be in a range of $90 million to $100 million, well below consensus expectations.

We are guiding with caution due to the continued changes in the macro environment, including tariff uncertainty as well as our near-term focus on operationalizing our partnership with Eaton.

While there is always a possibility of headwinds from deterioration in macro conditions, we expect revenue upside later in the year from the introduction of our new AC hardware that Rick outlined, better performance in Europe and growth from our new partnership with Eaton.

However, management still expects the company to achieve positive Adjusted EBITDA before the end of the current fiscal year.

Considering current market conditions, I do not expect the company to come even close to Adjusted EBITDA profitability this year.

In addition, substantial cash usage remains a cause for concern, particularly given the requirement to refinance $300 million of convertible notes ahead of their April 1, 2028 maturity date.

Clearly, my upgrade from "Sell" to "Hold" in early December following improved Q3/FY2025 results has been premature, as the stock has lost almost 50% of its value since that time:

Adding insult to injury, investors will likely have to prepare for a reverse stock split later this year in order to regain compliance with the New York Stock Exchange's continued listing standards (emphasis added by author).

On February 19, 2025, ChargePoint Holdings, Inc. was notified by the New York Stock Exchange that it is not in compliance with Section 802.01C of the NYSE Listed Company Manual because the average closing price of the Company's Common Stock was less than $1.00 over a consecutive 30 trading-day period. (...)

The Company intends to consider available alternatives, including but not limited to, a reverse stock split, subject to stockholder approval no later than at the Company's next annual meeting of stockholders, if necessary, to cure the stock price non-compliance.

The combination of a challenging operating environment, substantial cash usage, looming debt issues, ambitious near-term profitability targets and the likely requirement to conduct a reverse stock is causing me to downgrade ChargePoint Holdings' common stock back to "Sell".

Bottom Line

ChargePoint Holdings reported underwhelming first quarter results and provided disappointing Q2 guidance.

While management still targets Adjusted EBITDA profitability later this year, challenging market conditions are not likely to abate anytime soon.

In addition, persistent cash usage does not bode well for the company's ability to address its 2028 convertible notes.

Lastly, investors need to be wary of the likely requirement to conduct a reverse stock split later this year.

Considering the headwinds discussed above, I am downgrading my rating on ChargePoint Holdings' common shares back to "Sell" and apologize for a bad call in December.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Gabelli U.S. Treasury Money Market Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
SHAREHOLDER COMMENTARY

The U.S. bond market dealt with turbulence and uncertainty in the first quarter of 2025, shaped by changing expectations for interest rates, shifting economic data, and rising geopolitical tension, especially following the new administration's announcement of sweeping tariffs. Early in the quarter, Treasury yields stayed elevated as strong consumer spending and solid job growth supported the outlook for continued economic strength. That narrative began to shift in March, when the administration unveiled a 10% blanket tariff on all imports and added harsher penalties for key trade partners like China. The move raised fresh concerns about inflation, global trade disruptions, and slower growth.

In response, investors turned to U.S. Treasuries as a safe haven, causing yields to fall across the curve. The 2-year yield (US2Y), a key gauge of market expectations for Fed policy, dropped from just over 4.24% at the start of the year to 3.88% by the end of March. The 10-year yield (US10Y) followed suit, falling from 4.56% to about 4.21%. These moves reflected growing uncertainty about the economy's direction and increased speculation that the Federal Reserve may need to ease policy sooner than expected.

Throughout the quarter, the Fed held rates steady at 4.25% to 4.50%, while maintaining a wait-and-see approach. Policymakers acknowledged progress in bringing down inflation but emphasized the need for more evidence before moving toward rate cuts. In a sign of growing caution, the Fed also announced it would begin slowing the pace of balance sheet runoff in April, easing one of the more restrictive parts of its current policy stance.

Economic data continued to send mixed signals. March payrolls came in strong, adding 228,000 jobs, but the unemployment rate edged up to 4.2% and wage growth slowed to 3.8%. Inflation cooled slightly, with March's Consumer Price Index rising 2.4% year-over-year; still above the Fed's target but moving in the right direction. Meanwhile, consumer confidence weakened and business sentiment declined, especially among small businesses grappling with the uncertainty surrounding new trade policies.

The market's turning point came in late March with the tariff announcement. Equities fell, Treasury demand spiked, and rate expectations quickly shifted. Fed funds futures began to price in rate cuts as early as June, even as the Fed stuck to its cautious, data-driven messaging. Chair Powell reiterated the need for clarity and sustained signs of slowing inflation and growth before the Fed would act.

As the quarter closed, the bond market was caught between two possibilities: a soft landing, where inflation falls without a sharp slowdown in growth, or a recession, triggered by rising costs, tighter financial conditions, and trade-related shocks. While the economy is still on relatively solid footing, the path ahead is increasingly uncertain. Markets remain highly sensitive to incoming data and policy developments. For now, the bond market is signaling caution, watching closely to see whether the economy can glide toward stability or if it is heading for a more difficult landing.

MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION

During the first quarter of 2025, our investment strategy remained squarely focused on preserving liquidity. We closely monitored macroeconomic developments, Federal Reserve policy signals, and investor cash flow needs to guide positioning. Treasury bill yields held firm near the upper end of the Fed's target range, while government money market funds continued to attract steady inflows, highlighting investors' ongoing preference for safety and flexibility amid equity market volatility.

Expectations for potential rate cuts later this year contributed to a flatter front-end yield curve. In response to continued geopolitical uncertainty, softening economic data, and a growing outlook for monetary easing, we maintained a conservative stance. The portfolio remained actively allocated to 3 and 6-month Treasury bills, each with average yields of approximately 4.28%. Our barbell strategy, combining ultra-short maturities for immediate liquidity with slightly longer positions to capture relative value for longer, ensures the portfolio remains flexible, resilient, and well-positioned in a shifting rate environment.

Money market funds remained a key destination for capital, reflecting ongoing investor demand for safety, liquidity, and yield. As of March 31, 2025, total money market fund assets climbed to approximately $6.98 trillion, up from $6.85 trillion at year- end 2024. Government money market funds accounted for the vast majority of this growth, now totaling $5.70 trillion, while prime funds held approximately $1.05 trillion and tax-exempt funds stood at $180 billion. This allocation underscores continued investor preference for high-quality, low-volatility instruments amid ongoing uncertainty. The Gabelli U.S. Treasury MMF remains strategically positioned to navigate this environment. Our focus on short-term Treasury securities ensures that the portfolio is both liquid and resilient. We continue to actively manage maturities and reinvest cash flows in alignment with prevailing market conditions and anticipated Fed policy. As the Fed maintains a cautious, data-dependent stance, our barbell structure enables us to respond dynamically to changes in yield curve shape, monetary policy, and broader macro developments.

FUND PERFORMANCE

For the 3 months ending March 31, 2025 the Gabelli U.S. Treasury Money Market Fund produced a return of 4.37% annualized, with a 7-day yield of 4.23%. The Fund's investment objective is to provide current income while maintaining liquidity and a stable share price of $1.00. The Fund invests exclusively in U.S. Treasury securities. As such, it is considered one of the most conservative investment options offered within the Gabelli complex. Although the Fund invests in short-term U.S. Government securities, the amount of income that a shareholder may receive will be largely dependent on the current interest rate environment.

data.title: DocuSign Q1: Lowered Billing Growth Is Just Renewal Timing Issue
data.description:
I assigned a ‘Buy’ rating to DocuSign (NASDAQ:DOCU) in November 2024, highlighting its international expansion opportunities. The stock price took a hit when the company released its Q1 FY26 earnings due to lowered full-year billing guidance. While its billing growth decelerated during the quarter, I remain confident that DocuSign will deliver high-single-digit revenue growth in the near future. I reiterate the "Buy" rating, with a fair value of $95 per share.

DocuSign released its Q1 FY26 result on June 5th after the market close, posting 7.6% revenue growth and 11.4% adj. operating income growth. However, the billing growth decelerated to 4.2% during the quarter, as detailed in the table below:

As noted in the earnings remark, DocuSign made several go-to-market changes in order to reaccelerate its business growth and improve margins. These changes led to lower-than-expected renewal billings in FY26. As a result, its billing growth decelerated to 4.2% during the quarter, and the management lowered its full-year billing guidance to $3.285B- $3.339B, from the prior guidance of $3.3B to $3.354B. I agree with the management’s view that the lower billing growth is a timing issue, not a problem with demand.

As discussed in my previous article, DocuSign remains a US-centric business, with the US representing around 72% of total revenue. During the quarter, the company achieved 10% year-over-year growth in its international business, and prioritized the top 8 international markets with direct go-to-market strategies. I remain confident DocuSign’s international business will continue its growth momentum in the near future, contributing around 3% of growth to the overall topline, as per my estimate.

DocuSign continues to grow its Intelligent Agreement Management [IAM] platform, leveraging AI to optimize enterprise customers’ processes. During the call, the management highlighted that over 10k customers have purchased its IAM system, and the company will continue to pursue go-to-market strategies among small and mid-market customers.

As detailed in the slide below, the company is guiding for $3.151-$3.163 bn revenue with 27.8%-28.8% of adj. operating margin.

I forecast DocuSign will deliver 6% organic revenue growth in the near term, driven by 3% growth from existing customers renewals and 3% from new client acquisitions. I believe its IAM platform will become a major growth driver for DocuSign and the AI technology can fully integrate with DocuSign’s existing agreement platform. In addition, I anticipate the company will allocate 4% of revenue towards M&A, contributing an additional 1.3% growth to the topline. As a result, I calculate the company can deliver 7.3% of reported revenue growth in the near future.

I model 280bps annual margin expansion for the company, driven by 100bps from gross margin improvement; 100bps from operating leverage from SG&A and 80bps from reducing R&D costs. It’s worth noting that DocuSign has been implementing cloud migration, which has caused some additional one-time costs. The management anticipates the cost pressure will start to ease from FY27. I calculate the total operating costs will grow by 3.75% annually, leading to an operating margin of 35.7% by FY35.

DCF summary:

FCFF calculation:

WACC: 10.2%; assuming: risk-free 4.2%; beta 1; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $18 bn; debt 0; tax rate 19%.

Terminal growth: 4.5%; assuming 2% pricing growth and 2% usage growth.

Discounting all the FCFF and adding net cash, the fair value is calculated to be $95 per share, as detailed in the table below:

Key Risks

As discussed previously, DocuSign has made several changes to its sales team and go-to-market policies, including sales compensation, market territories and customer-size segmentations, as noted in the earnings call. While I think these sales & marketing changes are necessary for future growth, the sales and renewal disruptions could potentially last longer than the management anticipated. If the changes in the sale team cause some salesperson turnover, it would pose some near-term growth challenges to DocuSign.

Conclusion

I view the guidance cut for billings is a timing issue caused by their go-to-market policy changes. I reiterate the "Buy" rating, with a fair value of $95 per share.

data.title: Lululemon Q1 2025 Earnings Update
data.description:
While the quarter looks mostly okay, Lulu’s (NASDAQ:LULU) guide is the primary culprit for the stock to be down 20%+ after-hours today. Before we get into that, let me recap the quarter first, and then I will share some highlights from the call.

Sales by Region

Lulu is again back to LSD growth in the US; what makes it more disappointing is they actually had a somewhat easier comparison, since 1Q’24 growth was just 2%. Canada, despite a low double-digit growth in 1Q’24, again had higher growth than the US this quarter.

China and Rest of the World (RoW) maintained healthy growth rates, but growth has materially decelerated. However, due to the timing of the Chinese New Year, Lulu’s China growth was impacted by four points.

Given the US is still the majority of the business (albeit declining in overall mix; US as % of overall mix has been trending downward for a while: 68% in 1Q’22, 66% in 1Q’23, 61% in 1Q’24, and 57% in 1Q’25), the main focus is still around the anemic growth in the US.

What explains the struggle in the US? Management mostly blamed “macro uncertainty” and “consumer confidence” as the key reasons which led to traffic decline in stores. They mentioned conversion remains mostly consistent and average order value actually increased YoY.

While most management teams typically like to shift the blame to macro, I wonder if the real challenge here is the broader athleisure segment, which definitely took plenty of share in the overall apparel industry in the last few years post-Covid and now may be ceding some of the shares.

The reason I suspect this may be at least partly the case is Athleta’s persistent struggle as well which posted 1Q’25 revenue that was ~15% lower than what they did in 1Q’22. In contrast, Lulu’s revenue in the US was actually +24% during that same period.

In fact, Lulu management highlighted that they believe they continue to take share in the US:

When I look at our performance vs the market, we gained market share in the premium activewear. We had strong performance gains vs our peers in this segment of the market where we compete…We're definitely not happy where the growth is in the U. S. But relative to the market and our performance vs others, we are pleased that we're putting on share

Of course, we cannot track some of the key competitors such as Alo or Vuori since they aren’t public companies. Moreover, if the broader Athleisure segment is under pressure, we may see increased promotional activity or increased markdown in the US. Lulu management did confirm that they suspect the back half of the year is likely to experience that:

from a competitive perspective, there's nothing we're seeing globally on a price promotional play other than in the U.S., where I would say we continue to monitor that closely because we do see ongoing promotional activity across the market, across the competitors as we've seen the certain consumer the more cautious.We know that to lever other pulls and we continue to monitor it and quite frankly, anticipating a bit of a spike in the back half if the macro headwinds continue. But we are a full price business, and we'll lead with innovation and our core assortment, we'll continue to play that. But we are seeing and do anticipate probably a dynamic competitive market. in the U.S.

Lulu’s markdown so far is down 10 bps compared to last year, but they expect it to increase over the course of the year by 10-20 bps YoY. Management thinks traffic is the leading indicator when it comes to markdown and given the declining trend in traffic in the US (traffic hasn’t improved in QTD as well), they assume the overall markdown will rise in 2025 vs 2024.

Lack of newness was mentioned as the key reason for last year’s lackluster growth in the US, but they seem to have corrected the newness mix and plan to roll out some of the recently launched styles over the course of the year (which was launched in just a limited number of stores so far):

In terms of the composition of our merchandise mix, we are back at our newness percentages…I think the way the guest is reacting and responding within that newness, she is reacting very positively to the new core or intended core silhouette styles that she has not seen before… Align no line, the Daydrift, to Be Calm to name just a few, and there's a number of those.Those as a percentage of our newness mix, we are increasing in the back half so that we're reacting to what the guest is responding to, and as a result, we are shifting some of the seasonal colors, patterns and graphics in the remaining core to maintain that sort of ratio that we're seeing. But as I look to the back half, the percentage of newness remains strong above historical as we lean into a little bit of these areas where the guest has really responded well, and we weren't at full store distribution.

One good thing, however, is that management mentioned unaided brand awareness in the US increased from mid-30s in 4Q’24 to 40% in 1Q’25. Of course, we are not seeing such positivity in the financials yet, but this unaided brand awareness is critical for Lulu to eventually get back to healthy growth rates in their US business.

Operating Margin

In both the Americas and in China, Lulu’s operating margin improved YoY.

I will discuss tariff more in the outlook section, but Lulu thinks they can absorb some of these increased costs and likely pass some to consumers given the “elasticity” of their consumer base:

We have an industry leading operating margin. This allows us to continue investing across our strategic road map to enable long term growth while managing any increased cost associated with tariffs.our premium positioning in the performance athletic apparel category yields different elasticity for our products relative to fashion oriented brands.

Sales by Gender

All three segments grew at largely similar rates in 1Q’25.

Inventory

In 4Q’24 call, Lulu guided inventory to grow at high teens as they chase the newness mix, but inventory actually grew by 23%. However, management indicated this was mostly due to FX and tariff; when considering inventory in terms of units, it increased by 16% which was more in line with the guide.

When looking at inventory, we expect units to increase in the low double digits in Q2, with dollar inventories up in the low 20s and due in large part to the impact of higher tariff rates and foreign exchange. We expect a similar dynamic in inventory growth for the remainder of the year.

Capital Allocation

Lulu maintained their buyback intensity throughout last quarter. They bought back $430 mn shares at $316/share (vs. $332 Mn in 4Q’24 and $297 Mn in 1Q’24).

As a result, Lulu’s shares outstanding declined by 4.3% YoY in 1Q’25. Management’s buyback activity in the face of headwinds faced by the business does seem to project confidence about the long-term health of the business. Given they still have $1.3 Billion cash on balance sheet and the stock price reaction to this earnings, I expect them to remain quite active in buying back shares.

Outlook

For 2025, topline guide remains unchanged $11.15-11.3 Bn, implying ~5-7% YoY growth. If you exclude the 53rd week impact, this implies ~7-8% growth.

However, they lowered the EPS guide for 2025 from $14.95-15.15 to $14.58 to $14.78 (vs. $14.64 in 2024). Why was EPS guided lower despite keeping the topline guide?

For the full year, we now expect gross margin to decrease approximately 110 basis points versus 2024. Relative to our prior guidance for a 60 basis point decrease, we expect the additional 50 basis points of deleverage to be driven predominantly by increased tariffs, offset somewhat by our enterprise-wide efforts to mitigate these costs and slightly higher markdowns. When looking specifically at tariffs, the assumptions we've made regarding rates include 30% incremental tariffs on China and an incremental 10% on the remaining countries where we source.From a mitigation standpoint, as Calvin said, we've looked across the enterprise and have identified several levers which will help offset much of the impact of these higher rates. Based on our implementation strategies, we expect our mitigation efforts to be most impactful in the second half of the year.Turning to SG&A for the full year. We expect deleverage of approximately 50 basis points versus 2024, relatively in line with our prior guidance, driven by FX headwinds and ongoing investments into our Power 3x 2 road map, including investments in marketing and brand building aimed at increasing our awareness and acquiring new guests, investments to support our international growth and market expansion and continued investment in technology.Looking at operating margin for the full year 2025, we now expect a decrease of approximately 160 basis points versus 2024

So, basically, an additional ~50 bps pressure on the gross margin due to tariffs than what was communicated in the 4Q’24 call; the tariff was already assumed to be ~20 bps headwind during the 4Q’24 call, but of course, that was before the broad-based tariff that Trump later imposed. They do expect to raise prices a little in some assortments to minimize this impact, but nonetheless will take some margin hit.

When we think about the tariff impact to mitigation actions, I'd highlight, one would be pricing. We are planning to take strategic price increases looking item by item across our assortment as we typically do, and it will be price increases on a small portion of our assortment, and they will be modest in nature.And then on the sourcing side, we are also pursuing some efficiency actions there, some of which will impact the second half of this year, and then we are also focused on that into '26 as well.

Final Words

I bought Lulu in April 2024, so I clearly underestimated the challenges Lulu has been facing in the US. While it is possible that Lulu has been gaining share, there is lot of noise in verifying such a claim given I don’t have data related to Alo/Vuori, etc.

In any case, gaining share is no panacea if the segment itself is facing a headwind. The reality is, without persistent MSD-HSD growth in the US, Lulu will find it very difficult to get back to mid-20s P/E multiple (or higher).

It currently trades at ~17x P/E (based on the high end of their current 2025 EPS guide). I have mentioned last quarter that I don’t intend to add to my position unless Lulu trades at ~10x NTM EBIT, which is basically another 20% down from the current price in after-hours.

Given that I think Lulu will likely still manage to grow at MSD+ rate over the next 3-5 years even in the scenario that the US remains stuck in LSD growth, it is sufficiently attractive for me to start adding if the stock experiences a 20% drawdown from here.

Thank you for reading.

Disclosure: I own January 2026 $165 Call Options.

data.title: Spotify's Valuation Implies Subscriber Growth It Doesn't Have
data.description:
It has been over 18 months since I last covered Spotify Technology S.A. (NYSE:SPOT) for Seeking Alpha. At that time, I gave the stock a 'buy' rating on the company's 'reorientation' and headcount reduction. In my closing section of that piece, I mentioned how the platform's paid-sub growth was slowing and how that was problematic given paid subscriptions are the biggest driver of both top-line revenue and gross margin. Nevertheless, I still liked what I saw broadly:

Given premium subs are the biggest source of revenue, I'm not at all surprised to see leadership trimming headcount and getting leaner heading into 2024. As a long-term investment, I'm coming around on this company and have decided to upgrade from "hold" to "buy."

At that time, the stock was under $200 per share. Today, we find SPOT well over $700 per share. Given such an enormous run in the price of the stock, a reassessment is warranted.

Overall Performance & MAU Trends

Spotify's revenue in Q1-25 came in at €4,190 million and grew 15.2% year over year. Revenue was slightly down sequentially. As has typically been the case, the overwhelming majority of Spotify's top-line revenue comes from its premium subscriber tier.

Spending cuts have done the company wonders. The encouraging story from Spotify's financials is the growth in operating income and earnings. The company flipped to profitability in the second half of 2023 and has produced 5 consecutive quarters of positive earnings per share.

Digging a little deeper, the MAU trend is very interesting. I shared the same chart below in my December 2023 article covering the company. The trend in YoY growth of premium subscribers isn't all that surprising. That growth has been decelerating for years. However, what looks different with six additional quarters of subscriber data is the nosedive in the growth rate of even the ad-supported users:

The rate of YoY ad-supported subscriber growth has fallen from 34% in Q2-23 to just 9% in Q1-25. Furthermore, that growth rate for ad-supported users has been lower than paid-subscribers for two of the last three quarters. There are two ways to look at this. On one hand, paid users have been a much bigger driver of Spotify's profitability. The company's 33.5% gross margin on premium users was terrific last quarter. As was the 15.3% margin on ad-supported users.

At times, the company has taken a loss on those free users. Spotify has been willing to do this because the free users become part of the company's sales funnel and are easier to convert to paid subs than streamers who don't use the platform at all. On the other hand, this MAU trend could be indicative of a company that is getting closer to its peak user figures than investors may realize. If that is indeed the case, shareholders are perhaps looking at a rather significant valuation problem for SPOT shares.

Valuations Are Unattractive

Although I was long the stock back in December 2023 when I last covered it, Spotify's valuation was a concern of mine even back then. Though I noted, the stock wasn't nearly as overpriced as streaming pure play peers like Roku (ROKU) or Netflix (NFLX) based on a traditional price to sales multiple. That is no longer the case. While still not as 'overvalued' as Netflix, SPOT's forward PS ratio is not cheap.

The chart above shows the forward sales multiple, but it should be noted that the trailing twelve-month story is similar. At 7x forward sales, SPOT is quite extended in my personal view. And frankly, I don't think the admittedly better EPS story over the last 5 quarters justifies the current earnings multiples:

SPOT is now trading at 118x trailing earnings and just under 70x forward earnings. And again, this is with year over year revenue growth slowing, year over year subscriber growth slowing, and ad-supported users actually declining between Q4-25 and Q1-25. To get a sense for how much Spotify would have to be charging paid users for even a 30x earnings multiple at the current stock price, consider this data:

Spotify TTM actual Hypothetical
Revenue (m$) $17,556.0 $21,088.2
Earnings (m$) $1,261.5 $4,793.7
EPS $6.24 $23.70
Share Price $711 $711
P/E 113.9 30.0

Source: Seeking Alpha, Author's estimates

To justify a 30x earnings multiple at $711 per share, Spotify would have to essentially quadruple its trailing twelve-month earnings of $1.2 billion to $4.8 billion. Without slashing SG&A down to the studs, the most straightforward way to quadruple earnings would be to either grow top-line revenue through subscriber growth - which has been slowing - or to just monetize the current MAUs to a stronger degree. Which could be done either through raising prices on users who are already paying or increasing ad loads for the free tier.

My hunch is raising prices on premium subscribers would be the most viable way to do that given the ad-supported user trend we observe at the moment. Premium subscribers accounted for 90% of top-line revenue in Q1-25, and that number was actually slightly higher than Q1-24. As mentioned in the prior section, Premium sub growth is now higher than free user growth. This could imply that free users are upgrading, Spotify is approaching peak MAUs, or both.

Revenue Q1-24 Q4-24 Q1-25 YoY QoQ
Premium €3,247 €3,705 €3,771 16.1% 1.8%
Ad-Supported €389 €537 €419 7.7% -22.0%
% from Premium 89.3% 87.3% 90.0% 0.8% 3.0%
Premium Subs 239 263 268 12.1% 1.9%
Ad-Supported Users 388 425 423 9.0% -0.5%
Premium ARPU €13.59 €14.09 €14.07 3.6% -0.1%
Ads ARPU €1.00 €1.26 €0.99 -1.2% -21.6%

Source: Spotify, author's calculations, non-currency figures in millions

Not only did ad-based subscribers dip last quarter, but revenue per user from the ad-supported tier slipped 1.2% year over year as well. All this said, Spotify can get to the $4.8 billion in hypothetical full year earnings by raising prices across the board by roughly 20%. Either that, or the company could launch higher tiers as new offerings - which is something that has come up during conference calls. But short of that, SPOT is expensive at its current valuation, and growing into that valuation through earnings seems less likely than a simple stock price re-rating lower from where I sit. And I'd be remiss if I didn't mention that even at 30x earnings, SPOT might still be overvalued if the subscriber growth story is indeed nearing maturity.

Closing Summary

I'm not bearish Spotify whatsoever. Audio books, podcasting, video, and growth through more premium tiers are all compelling avenues for the company. Though I should note paid video streaming services are likely already at a saturation point. But I do like the business long term, I just don't like the price I'd be paying for it today. I'm downgrading SPOT shares from 'buy' to 'hold' but would certainly entertain buying the stock again if/when the share price comes back down to earth.

data.title: Mairs & Power Balanced Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview | First Quarter 2025

In the first quarter of 2025, the S&P 500 Total Return (TR) was -4.27%, the Russell 2000 (TR) was -9.48%, and the Bloomberg U.S. Government/Credit Bond Index was 2.70%.

The year began positively for the stock market, reaching an all-time high in February, but tariff uncertainty in March caused a decline in equities while fixed income benefited from a flight to perceived safety.

Despite the headline indices declining, a deeper look at sector performance of the S&P 500 reveals significant variation in first quarter performance. Growth stocks, particularly in the Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology sectors, saw the sharpest declines, as their valuation levels retreated after a long stretch of outperformance. In response, traditionally “defensive” stocks, namely in Health Care, Consumer Staples, and Utilities, led the market.

While the stock market experienced disruption, the economy continued to show some resilience. The labor market remains healthy, adding 228,000 jobs in March, and the unemployment rate is still low at 4.2%. Additionally, consumer spending and housing market activity improved in recent months, after lulls to begin the year. Manufacturing also showed signs of picking up, with the ISM survey finally rising above 50, indicating expansion. Although this data is now “old news,” it paints a picture of an economy that entered the current upheaval on somewhat stable footing.

However, prospects of a prolonged trade war with China raise concerns about reigniting inflation and slowing economic growth. The uncertainty created by this backdrop clouds decision-making, which is already flashing warning signs with declining consumer and business confidence. The University of Michigan’s Index of Consumer Sentiment fell in March to its lowest level since 2022, and business confidence surveys also weakened.

When faced with prolonged uncertainty, businesses and consumers become more cautious, delaying spending, investment, and hiring decisions. This can lead to a slowdown in economic activity, as decisions to commit resources are paused. The Atlanta Federal Reserve’s GDPNow model, which provides a real-time gauge of economic activity, deteriorated sharply in March, signaling a possible contraction in first quarter GDP.

The potential impact on inflation from tariffs is another cause for concern. While inflation has receded from pandemic highs, it has proven stubborn at 2.8% and is a key source of consumer frustration. At its core, tariffs are a tax, one that either businesses must absorb (at the expense of lower margins), or consumers must absorb by paying higher prices (leading to lower consumption). We expect many businesses to pass on most of this tax to consumers, which would likely create additional near-term upward pressure on prices and inflation. This dynamic of slowing growth and rising inflation (stagflation) puts the Federal Reserve in a difficult position, casting more uncertainty on future interest rate decisions.

Future Outlook

Given the complexity of global supply chains and unknown future responses, we cannot predict what is next, but we know it has created a fog-like uncertainty pervading the economic outlook. Uncertainty is a fact of life, but the current environment certainly creates a more challenging landscape for policymakers and businesses to navigate. The longer uncertainty persists, the harder it is for companies and consumers to make long-term decisions, which ultimately leads to slower economic growth. We expect more volatility in the months ahead as policies and their impact become clearer.

As economic concerns mount, the outlook for corporate earnings is looking more subdued. Current expectations for first quarter S&P 500 profits point to 7.3% growth, but that is down from the 12% growth that was expected at the beginning of the year. A similar trend is occurring with full-year 2025 estimates, which have declined from 12.7% growth to 10.6% growth. Although earnings are still expected to grow this year, estimates are moving lower, and the sharp pullback in stock prices suggests investors are becoming increasingly nervous about the outlook.

At the same time, valuation levels are also contracting, reflecting the murkier outlook for the market. During the first quarter, the S&P 500 forward 12-month price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio fell from 22 times to just over 20 times, in line with the 5-year average though still above the 10-year average of 18.3. Meanwhile, the S&P Small Cap 600 is trading at 14.3 times earnings, a 30% discount to the S&P 500, and well below its 10-year average of 16.7. The recent correction has removed some of the froth from the market, particularly for the large cap growth stocks, with the S&P Equal Weighted P/E now back to its 10-year average of 16.5 times earnings.

Having a sound investment philosophy is always important, but even more so when market conditions become challenging. Rather than investing in markets, our approach of investing in companies with durable business models, healthy balance sheets, and reasonable valuations lends itself to the current environment. We remain committed to this philosophy and will do what we’ve always done, looking for opportunities that we believe will benefit our clients over the long term.

Performance Review

The Mairs & Power Balanced Fund finished the first quarter of 2025 up 0.04%. The Fund outperformed both the benchmark composite index (60% S&P 500 Total Return Index and 40% Bloomberg U.S. Government/Credit Bond Index), which was down 1.48%, and the Morningstar Moderate Allocation peer group, which fell 0.34%.

Performance data quoted represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance of the Fund may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. For the most recent month-end performance figures, please call Shareholder Services at (800) 304-7404. Expense Ratio 0.71%.

The Fund continues to have an overweight to equities which negatively impacted performance as stocks underperformed bonds during the period. After setting new record highs in early 2025, equities slipped into the end of the quarter as general concerns about tariffs dragged on sentiment. The bonds in the Balanced Fund had positive low-single-digit total returns as lower rates offset widening credit spreads.

As discussed in previous quarters, the last two years witnessed a narrow grouping of stocks driving a significant portion of stock market returns. However, the market reversed course with five of the “Magnificent 7” experiencing double digit declines during the quarter. The Fund’s underweight to this grouping in the first quarter delivered much of the Fund’s relative outperformance on equities.

The Information Technology sector underweight had the largest relative impact on returns during the quarter. Only one of the Fund’s technology holdings posted a positive quarterly return – Qualcomm (QCOM) – making this a somewhat hollow outperformance. Qualcomm is a “value tech” company which has enviable cash flows but we believe has been consistently underappreciated in the market for its technology licensing business, which has frequently been targeted for legal action by some of its major customers . However, we believe it continues to prove its best-in-class technology and, despite companies attempting to circumvent Qualcomm, we continue to see a long-term position in the wireless technology space for the company. Our preference for stable cash flow companies led to additional outperformance in the sector, with heavy overweights to Texas Instruments (TXN) and Motorola Solutions (MSI) benefiting relative returns. Texas Instruments has been on a multi- year business transition, investing heavily in semiconductor manufacturing facilities which are based in the U.S. Domestic facilities combined with general industrial end market weakness has kept the company from experiencing the same excitement as many of the other semiconductor companies. Motorola Solutions sells equipment and services for public safety, and it has experienced a consistent demand profile which has made it a favorable name in our technology portfolio for some time.

The Fund’s overweight to the Health Care sector significantly contributed to relative outperformance which was further aided by positive selection within the sector. Abbott Laboratories (ABT), a long-term portfolio holding, had its sixth quarter in a row of double-digit organic growth, while the market is increasingly appreciating the new trajectory for the diversified healthcare company. Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY), another long-term holding, had increasingly positive news around several of its in-pipeline drugs. Offsetting this, Bio- Techne (TECH) underperformed due to negativity around the funding environment for biopharmaceuticals and biotech research given recent federal funding cuts and a weak venture capital funding environment. We have taken advantage of the recent weakness to add to our Bio-Techne (TECH) position.

An underweight in Consumer Discretionary aided performance during the quarter.

Although the Fund’s sole holding in the sector, Home Depot (HD), outperformed the sector with a negative return, the main reason for outperformance was the complete absence of Amazon (AMZN) and Tesla (TSLA) from portfolio holdings.

The Fund’s holdings in the Communications Services sector dragged on portfolio returns during the quarter due primarily to security selection. Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG) led underperformance as it fell in tandem with the other mega cap tech stocks. Additionally, there is increasing concern about the impact of generative AI on Alphabet’s search business and whether it will be able to meaningfully respond. Disney (DIS) also underperformed both the market and the sector as expectations for its economically sensitive parks business decline, although the company’s underlying profitability has improved. Also, within the sector we have historically not held any of the telecom players who have had a more difficult time developing a sustainable competitive advantage.

During the quarter, the Fund started a new position in Wec Energy Group (WEC). WEC, a public utility headquartered in Wisconsin, operates in one of the most balanced regulatory environments for a utility and its stable service area led us to add the name early in the quarter. Additionally, the Fund is overweight equities, and WEC provides a quasi-fixed income holding in utilities, by which we mean the utility has a limited return profile within its regulatorily defined returns.

At the beginning of 2025, the fixed income markets were conflicted between expectations for pro-growth policies and that the Federal Reserve would keep cutting interest rates. However, the narrative around fixed income has shifted to a tradeoff between continued inflation and concerns around tariffs and a potential recession. This led interest rates to decline, with the 10-year falling from 4.57% to 4.21% at quarter end. However, credit spreads generally rose throughout the quarter with increasing uncertainty surrounding tariffs and international relations. Still, positioning on the curve, combined with credit selection, helped the portfolio slightly outperform even though the portfolio is short duration and overweight corporates. We have been raising credit quality in the portfolio in small increments given the low credit spreads that have prevailed until recently. We will continue to execute our strategy in fixed income, whereby we seek pockets of value and selectively research and buy bonds to help us achieve long-term outperformance.

Our equity style leads us to select companies with durable competitive advantages and attractive long-term growth prospects as this generally leads to sustainable investment returns. We seek to enter and build positions in companies at reasonable valuations. With the recent equity twists-and-turns, the opportunities to build these positions at attractive valuations are improving. Additionally, new layers of analysis are required with the shifting geopolitical dynamics. Notably, we have long had a general preference for domestic equities and domestic fixed income, which we believe leaves us less exposed to some emerging risks. While we do not make big calls on interest rates or credit cycles, we have increasingly positioned the fixed income to be less volatile in a credit cycle and continually work to improve the portfolio for the long run. This should allow us to be opportunistic in the marketplace and take advantage of dislocations brought on by periods of turmoil. We believe our consistent investment approach to both stocks and bonds will deliver sustainable performance over the long haul.

The price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio measures a company’s current share price relative to its per-share earnings. 10

data.title: Weibo: AI Bet Faces The Test Of Relevance In A Shifting Social Media Landscape
data.description:
Weibo (NASDAQ:WB) reported 1Q25 results, with revenue largely in line with consensus and net margin slightly beat.

Revenue was largely flat y/y as both key segments saw a stagnating growth profile. Notably, advertising revenue of $339mn was flat y/y while VAS revenue was $57.7mn, +2% y/y.

If we were to exclude revenue contribution from Alibaba, ad revenue of $296.5mn was +6% y/y from last year since ad and marketing revenue from Alibaba saw +89% y/y to $42.6mn due to BABA's strong promotional push around Chinese New Year. Non-GAAP net income attributable to Weibo's shareholders was $119.5mn, which was 26% higher than the consensus due to lower S&M and R&D expenses that were offset by lower gross margin and higher G&A expenses.

In terms of sector contribution, online gaming, handset, beauty, and personal care, saw a minor revenue decline. VAS revenue grew 2% y/y, mostly due to membership services. Management guided online ad revenue to see flat y/y growth in Q2, driven by auto advertising and ecommerce from the 618 promotion.

Our view on WB is that we question its relevance in the new social media era, as this product is largely a relic of the Internet 2.0 era. WB's product has largely been unchanged over the past decade, and we are concerned about the growing competition within social media content, given the proliferation of short-form videos and lifestyle-focused apps such as Redbook to take engagements away from WB.

WB's operating metrics in both DAU and MAU have been growing sequentially, with improvements across key metrics and engagement level.

However, the pace of growth has been decelerating on a sequential basis, which is showing signs of constraint on engagement user growth.

We remain cautious on the stock, which has not moved up since our last publication. However, we are upgrading this stock to HOLD from our prior SELL rating given the low valuation and high dividend payout is likely to provide support for this name.

We would be more bullish on the stock if operating metrics start to see signs of acceleration, coupled with an improvement in the macro backdrop that drives the topline fundamentals.

We would be more bearish on the stock if we see further deterioration in the macro backdrop, driven by rising Sino-US tensions and China's internally challenged economic landscape, which could weigh on ad spending. Additionally, deteriorating user engagement could ultimately hurt its ad growth outlook.

We see the risk/reward to be reasonably balanced, and hence we upgrade this stock to HOLD from SELL.

Riding on AI to drive product relevancy

WB has been incorporating AI to drive both user engagement and empower advertising, and remains relevant and competitive in the era of numerous social media and digital content options.

On the AI search side, WB leveraged LLMs such as DeepSeek to improve its search capabilities and user experience, which partially contributed to the MAU and DAU growth. Management noted that search has been particularly effective on news and entertainment content as well as trending topics, but there is still room for improvement in general search, which WB hopes to improve further to drive engagement.

User consumption of search has been positive, with a 5% incremental growth in per capita search volume. However, the issue with this lies in monetization. Our negative view on BIDU is that we believe BIDU may face a disintermediation risk due to the emergence of AI apps that could replace search, making it more difficult for BIDU to monetize its traditional search model. WB's general search could see similar monetization risk given the proliferation of alternative AI apps available in China that are free of charge.

On the advertising side, management cited that integration of AI has driven a 10% increase in CTR, while eCPM has increased by 5%. However, information feed ads on WB accounts for a small percentage of total ads, and this may not have a material impact on the topline growth.

Precarious outlook on ad trend

WB's ad outlook remains mixed, with management expecting overall ad performance to remain similar to Q1, with continued strength in automotive and e-commerce, partially driven by the 618 promotions, along with the national subsidy program.

However, key sectors like gaming and beauty/personal care continue to face headwinds, with reduced ad budgets from major players and y/y decline in spending.

We remain cautious about the impact of AI on driving meaningful revenue growth, despite early signs of measurable improvement in ad conversion. However, we are more cautious on the trajectory of China's macro backdrop that could negatively impact WB's ad outlook should the Sino-US tension or China's internal economic environment worsen from the current period.

Valuation looks balanced

WB currently trades at 5x forward P/E and 1.2x forward EV/Revenue, given its flat revenue and earnings growth outlook. The company also has net cash of $ 200 million against a market capitalization of $2.2 billion.

WB's valuation is in line with that of other advertising/content companies in China, such as Baidu, Bilibili, and Kuaishou, which also trade around 1.2x - 1.5x forward revenue multiple, and have a similar growth outlook of single-digit revenue growth.

If we were to value WB in line with its peers at 1.3x forward revenue multiple, we derive a target price of $10/share, or 8% upside from the current level, which we feel is not attractive enough to justify a BUY rating on the stock.

In short, we see a balanced risk-reward for WB at the current valuation, where the upside is limited given the flat revenue growth and uncertain macro outlook, while the downside is mitigated by the low valuation and the 8% dividend yield. We upgrade our rating from SELL to HOLD in light of the Q1 print as we await further upside or downside narrative to play out for us to change our view.

data.title: My Positive Outlook On Tesla Stock Paid Off, But It's Overvalued Now
data.description:
A lot has happened since my Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA)(TSX:TSLA:CA) article titled “Tesla Stock Has Corrected, But the Key Problems Facing It Remain Unchanged.” The stock has surged by more than 50% since the date of my article’s publication. Do not take me wrong. I was not bullish about the company, nor am I bullish now. But the selloff at the time was irrational. Let me explain why and how the situation around Tesla’s stock has changed.

My previous article about Tesla

In my previous article about Elon Musk’s corporation, I wrote that Tesla’s stock was down for political reasons, not fundamental ones linked to the corporation’s success or lack thereof. Tesla’s Elon Musk took on the position of the head of the DOGE department responsible for the US government’s efficiency. Investors and consumers supporting the US Democratic Party protested against this, which made TSLA stock crash.

Despite the stock price crash, I wrote that TSLA stock was still not a bargain from a fundamental viewpoint but gained substantial upside potential as a result of a rather irrational selloff.

The corporation’s business and financial fundamentals, namely falling profits and low sales growth, still remained unchanged.

Since my last article’s publication, not much has changed as far as the corporation’s fundamentals are concerned. But TSLA stock gained almost a whopping 50%

Also, it has recently been announced that Elon Musk has left his position as the head of the DOGE. In this article, I am going to cover the corporation’s recent news, its fundamentals, and its valuations.

Tesla’s news

So, why did the corporation’s stock price gain that much?

First, the market is expecting Tesla’s robotaxi launch in Austin.

The EV maker is expected to launch 10 to 20 self-driving Model Ys equipped with the latest version of Tesla’s full self-driving software. Earlier, the company tested the driverless car’s performance on Austin’s public roads.

Tesla has announced June 12 as the potential launch date of its robotaxi service in Austin.

The corporation plans to rapidly expand its self-driving fleet. According to analysts, it can potentially reach up to 1,000 vehicles within a few months and eventually extend the service to other cities like Los Angeles and San Francisco. For now, Tesla's robotaxi service is expected to use existing consumer models before launching an EV known as Cybercab, possibly as early as 2026.

A successful robotaxi launch in Texas could take the investors’ attention away from the corporation’s poor deliveries and falling sales revenues. Earlier in April, when Tesla’s deliveries and earnings reports were published, both the company’s sales and earnings disappointed investors. In the next section of this article, I will analyze them in the historical context.

Investors’ optimism is also due to the fact Elon Musk has recently left his position as the head of the DOGE. This made many analysts more hopeful that Musk would focus more on running Tesla’s business while moving away from politics. Also, in their opinion, both the company’s and the CEO’s reputations would only gain from the news.

Tesla’s earnings history

As you can see from the excerpt above coming from Tesla’s earnings report, the recent quarterly sales have substantially dropped. This is even truer of the corporation’s automotive revenues.

The company has reported the worst net income since 4Q 2020.

This can be well seen from the net income graph below.

The last reported deliveries were the worst since 2Q 2022. The poor earnings results have been due to both Tesla’s EV discounts and poor car sales.

Tesla’s quarterly deliveries

This can be well seen from the graph above. The corporation’s quarterly deliveries growth has, in fact, been negative over the period of three years. This is obviously serious for Tesla, given the fact that it is primarily an EV maker and its stock is trading like a high-growth rock star in terms of valuations. I will discuss this later in this article.

Tesla’s fundamentals

According to Seeking Alpha’s data, Tesla’s balance sheet position is still sound because it has negative net debt, meaning that its cash reserves exceed the liabilities it has to pay. The coverage ratio, the number of times Tesla can pay its interest, is more than 19, suggesting the company can easily service its debt. However, for a corporation with a market cap of more than $1 trillion, Tesla seems to have too little cash. According to Seeking Alpha, it’s just $37 billion. The company’s low debt is almost 15 times higher than its free cash flow [FCF], meaning that the company burns too much cash.

In 1Q 2025, the FCF merely totaled$664 million, substantially less compared to 2Q 2024, 3Q 2024, and 4Q 2024. So, I would say that in addition to falling sales and earnings, Tesla is also actively burning cash. However, Tesla is almost free of debt, which is a big advantage, of course.

Valuations

As we can see from the graph below, the TSLA stock price is currently below the peak reached in January this year. However, it has substantially surged since the February-March crash. In my opinion, given the deteriorating earnings and delivery results, the stock valuations do not reflect the fundamentals. In other words, TSLA stock seems to be overvalued.

The valuation ratios below presented by Seeking Alpha confirm this.

As you can see from the table above, all of the company’s valuation ratios are substantially higher than the industry’s averages. Almost all the company’s ratios, including Tesla’s price-to-earnings ratios, are ten times higher than the sector’s medians. I understand the company’s valuation premiums are due to the perceived innovation potential. After all, everyone expects the company to greatly benefit from “full self-driving,” Cybertrucks, artificial intelligence, and rising solar battery sales.

Conclusion

Thanks to the potential launch date of its robotaxi service, Tesla’s stock is trading quite high compared to the lows reached in February-March. Moreover, the fact Elon Musk left his position as the head of the DOGE also adds positively to the CEO’s reputation. However, Tesla’s earnings and deliveries fundamentals remain mediocre, while the business is burning cash. Given this, TSLA stock is overvalued.

data.title: Churchill Downs: Strong Buy Driven By Margin Expansion And Capital Discipline
data.description:
Margin Momentum Unlocked

We initiate on Churchill Downs Incorporated (NASDAQ:CHDN) at Strong Buy with a $131 PT. CHDN is a leading U.S. racing, online wagering, and gaming operator across three fast-growing segments: Live and Historical Racing, TwinSpires, and Gaming. Our Strong Buy is based on three conviction drivers:

1) We model $6.45 and $7.30 of diluted EPS for FY25E and FY26E (13% annualized and meaningfully ahead of consensus), driven by the company's $900mn Skye/Conservatory hold, which materially reduces capital charges and unlocks >$0.30 of incremental annual EPS by FY26E.

2) Management is accelerating high-margin HRM expansion in VA, with ramping The Rose and additional Richmond/Henrico capacity driving a 37% machine count increase at 45-50% incremental EBITDA margins.

3) The $500mn share repurchase will retire 4-5% of shares in 18 months, compounding a strong per-share profile. Market focus on transitory Derby ticket softness/1Q headwinds misses the improving core profitability at CHDN, and trading at the 32nd historical percentile for P/E, we see our 18.0x 2026 forward target multiple as well-supported.

Key risks include market caution on sector multiples/near-term event volatility, which we see as largely manageable vs. the upside. Bottom line: our high conviction is driven by a unique combination of above-consensus earnings growth, margin expansion, and capital returns, which we see as attractive from a R/R perspective for forward-looking investors.

Margin Expansion Unlocked By Capital Discipline

For sophisticated investors in search of underappreciated catalysts, Churchill Downs Incorporated’s capital allocation pivot is nothing less than transformational. The opportunity is massive: by strategically pausing the high-cost, multi-year Skye / Conservatory / Infield projects at Churchill Downs Racetrack (originally budgeting $900mn), management has irrevocably sharpened the company’s margin trajectory at the exact same moment when consensus remains laser-focused on event-driven volatility. Here’s the capital allocation trail: CHDN has reduced its 2025 project capex forecast by a remarkable $100–110mn, with total 2025 project spend now guided at a mere $250–290mn and maintenance capital reduced by $10mn to $90–100mn.

The financial impact is immediate, dramatic, and real: 1Q free cash flow jumped to $234mn, or $3.15 per share, providing the dry powder for aggressive buybacks and further leverage paydown. This deliberate capital pullback is not an exercise in waiting out macro uncertainty — it is sheer capital discipline, allowing the company to sidestep cost inflation risk while prioritizing smaller, higher-return investments such as the $25–30mn Finish Line Suites and Mansion upgrades, which are on track to deliver robust, low-risk shareholder value.

Virginia HRM buildout, the margin driver that underpins Churchill's next leg up

The opportunity comes into focus when isolating CHDN's hyper-focused rolling out of high-margin HRMs in Virginia, a move that flips the EBITDA comp equation. In '25, The Rose Gaming Resort opened with 1,650 HRMs + WPD grew 20% MoM through Feb, highlighting strong latent demand in the 3rd largest domestic gaming market (ex. Vegas). Not a one-and-done, Churchill's HRM penetration is scaling: VA installed base increased from 2.3k at acquisition to 4.45k in mid-'25, with a clear glidepath to the legal cap of 5.0k before YE (via imminent expansions in Richmond/Henrico). This is a transformative EBITDA lever: VA HRM venues generated $129.3mn in Q1 '25 revenues (+16% y/y) and contributed ~24% of total company EBITDA generation annually (launch drag from upfront marketing + weather volatility).

The kicker: new HRM units are modeled at 45-50% incremental EBITDA margins, and analysts see the segment as contributing a game-changing 41% of total property-level EBITDA in '26E (as HRM EBITDA grows 60% from '23 to '26E). The flywheel is real: as regional gaming faces cyclical headwinds and Derby volatility persists, the recurring, high-margin HRM cash flows turbocharge FCF/delevering. Bottom line: The VA HRM buildout is not just accretive growth — it is a transformational margin engine and structural cash flow driver that the market is missing, setting up for significant upside for the patient investor.

CHDN Above-Cons EPS Trajectory on HRM Leverage, Buyback Acceleration, Sharply Lower Cap Charges

We estimate FY25E/FY26E diluted EPS of $6.45/$7.30, vs. the street's $6.08/$6.73, reflecting 13% annualized EPS growth (vs. ~7-11% consensus) — a disconnect that, we believe, is meaningfully underappreciated by the market. We are driving this above consensus trajectory on

(1) strategic pause on $900mn Skye/Conservatory project, driving outsized interest and depreciation reduction, unlocking +$0.30+ of annual EPS by '26;

(2) substantial high-margin HRM capacity acceleration across VA, driven by the Rose's improving performance and capacity additions at Richmond and Henrico, driving +37% machine count mat and 45-50% incremental EBITDA margins; and

(3) aggressive $500mn share buyback program in place at depressed valuation levels, driving 4-5% share count reduction within 12–18 months of the program.

We believe the street is fixated on softness in Q1 and ticket demand at Derby, though we see a transitory weakness. Derby-151 is running in-line and the weather is normalizing from Q2. Yes, we concede that the street could remain unimpressed on the upside if it remains focused on sector multiples, but we are above the street on fundamentals in FY25E and FY26E.

Valuation

Our $131 PT is set by applying an 18.0x FY26E P/E multiple to our above-consensus FY26E EPS of $7.30 (42% upside). The target multiple is defensible given CHDN's estimated 13.2% EPS CAGR (vs peer/consensus), enabled by the Skye/Conservatory pause ($900mn) and Virginia's accelerated and high margin HRMs (both material capital charge relief) with the $500mn buyback providing incremental EPS leverage and substantial confidence in the intrinsic value with management's strong involvement.

Our target multiple is conservative considering the 21.2x historical median (32nd pctile) and a large discount to the historical 87% premium vs peers when our forward P/E is only modestly above the depressed peer median of 13.2x. Critically, while the market is fixated on transient Derby softness, the overlooked catalyst by consensus, in our view, is structurally leveraged margins from capital discipline compounding through the cycle. We view sector multiple caution and transitory headwinds over-discounted and CHDN's re-rating underappreciated.

Risks to Our Strong Buy thesis

Despite high conviction in unique margin expansion and capital return prospects, our CHDN thesis would be undermined by a tougher-than-expected macro or regulatory environment compounding through the remainder of the year, resulting in an enduring decline in regional gaming demand—particularly among the unrated/low-end market segment (a negative trend management just highlighted following Q1 results and the subsequent decline in the share price).

Similarly, current outsized capital discipline appears largely dependent on the suspension of the $900mn Skye/Conservatory project, as ongoing concerns over real or perceived trade wars, inflationary pressures, and tariff-inflated labor & materials costs on construction projects create an increasingly longer trail. The resulting erosion of long-term Kentucky Derby and hospitality earnings ramp would necessitate a strategic reevaluation of capital allocation priorities in light of outsized growth sensitivity.

Finally, CHDN's ongoing expansion of HRM and online wagering remains the centerpiece of our bullish thesis, but any negative regulatory developments (taxation, licensing, legal, etc.) could constrain margin leverage/growth potential, particularly in light of recent regulatory headwinds in MI for TwinSpires. This includes potential developments in VA or KY, or headline controversies.

We outline our key risk scenarios in context of the aforementioned outlined investment thesis above, and highlight the following fundamental data points to gauge the trajectory of risk events to inform valuation: 1) Monthly gaming/HRM revenue trends; 2) Derby-week revenue results; 3) State legislative actions impacting HRMs and online wagering; and 4) Real-time operating cost pressures.

Conclusion

We believe that Churchill Downs offers a compelling opportunity at current levels as margin momentum and capital discipline catalyze a sustained rerate that is underappreciated relative to the market's near-term focus on Derby volatility. With the Skye/Conservatory pause unlocking incremental EPS and management accelerating high-margin HRM expansion in Virginia, we see the core earnings trajectory and capital return profile as significantly underappreciated. For forward-looking investors, we view the current discounted entry point as a compelling risk/reward to build positions ahead of consensus as it incorporates structural higher profitability.
